revision.comment: 新しいページ：カナヅカ古墳（奈良県明日香村にある古墳）。
revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称 = カナヅカ古墳 |画像 = |別名 = 平田岩屋古墳 |所属 = |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 28|緯度秒 = 6.45 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 48|経度秒 = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |形状 = [[方墳]] |規模 = 一辺35m |埋葬施設 = 両袖式[[横穴式石室]] |出土品 = |陪塚 = |築造時期 = [[7世紀]]後半 |被葬者 = （一説）[[吉備姫王]] |陵墓 = 宮内庁治定（「[[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]」の陪冢） |史跡 = |有形文化財 = |特記事項 = |地図 = Japan Nara |アイコン = 方墳 |ラベル = カナヅカ
古墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} '''カナヅカ古墳'''（カナヅカこふん、平田岩屋古墳）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[方墳]]。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、[[梅山古墳]]（[[欽明天皇]]陵）と[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]との間において、丘陵南斜面をコ字形にカットして造成された平坦面上に築造された古墳である。[[江戸時代]]には石室が開口し、[[明治]]期には石室が破壊を受けているほか、[[1995年]]度（[[平成]]7年度）以降に範囲確認調査が実施されている{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 墳丘は2段築成{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳形は方形で、一辺約35メートルを測る{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘前面には東西約60メートル・南北約25メートルを測る大型のテラス面が構築される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘周囲には周濠が巡らされており、北側で幅11.7メートルを測る{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。主体部の埋葬施設は両袖式の[[横穴式石室]]で、南方向に開口した{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室全長約16メートルを測る大型石室であり、石材には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。副葬品は詳らかでない。 このカナヅカ古墳は、[[古墳時代]][[終末期古墳|終末期]]の[[7世紀]]後半頃の築造と推定される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。被葬者は明らかでないが、梅山古墳（欽明天皇陵）との位置関係などから欽明天皇皇孫女の[[吉備姫王]]の真墓とする説が挙げられている（現墓は梅山古墳南西に所在）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 === 来歴 === * [[江戸時代]] ** [[文化 (元号)|文化]]初年、河内秀根が『書紀集解』に梅山古墳の陪冢の「金冢」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[文政]]12年（[[1829年]]）、津川長道が『卯花日記』に「金塚」として石室露出を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[嘉永]]元年（[[1848年]]）、北浦定政が『打墨縄』に「金塚」として[[吉備姫王]]墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** 嘉永7年（[[1854年]]）、津久井清影が『聖蹟図志』に「岩屋」として石室開口の様子を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[安政]]4年（[[1857年]]）、谷森善臣の『大和國山陵廻之日記』に「金冢」として吉備姫王墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[慶応]]3年（[[1867年]]）、陵墓図に「字金塚檜隈坂合陵」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1890年]]（[[明治]]23年）、[[三宅米吉]]が破壊に遭遇（[[喜田貞吉]]が記録）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1892年]]（明治25年）、[[宮内省]]（現・[[宮内庁]]）により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1995年|1995]]-[[1997年]]度（[[平成]]7-9年度）、範囲確認調査（明日香村教育委員会、[[1996年|1996]]・1997・[[1998年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1999年]]（平成11年）、明治期の破壊当時の様子を記す資料の発見{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 埋葬施設 == 主体部の埋葬施設としては両袖式[[横穴式石室]]が構築されており、南方向に開口した{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室の規模は次の通り{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * 石室全長：約16メートル * 玄室：長さ約5.45メートル、幅約3.64メートル、高さ約2.7メートル * 羨道：長さ約10メートル、幅約1.8メートル、高さ約1.8メートル 石室には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石の切石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。壁面は玄室では2段積み（奥壁3石：上段1石・下段2石、両側壁4石：上段2石・下段2石）、羨道では1段積み（両側壁10石）である{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。また天井石は玄室で2石、羨道で3石から構成される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 石室の形態としては[[岩屋山古墳]]（[[明日香村]]）と同様の特徴を有しており、「岩屋山式石室」と捉えられるが、岩屋山古墳よりも後出し、[[峯塚古墳]]（[[天理市]]）からもやや後出する{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=西光慎治|year=2000|chapter=飛鳥地域の地域史研究(1) 檜隈坂合陵・陪冢 カナヅカ古墳の覚書|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf 明日香村文化財調査研究紀要 創刊号]}}|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|西光慎治|2000}}}} - リンクは明日香村ホームページ。 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=岡林孝作|year=2002|chapter=カナヅカ古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典|続 日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * 調査報告 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=カナヅカ古墳（第1次）範囲確認調査|title=明日香村遺跡調査概報 平成7年度|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=カナヅカ古墳（第2次）範囲確認調査|title=明日香村遺跡調査概報 平成8年度|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=カナヅカ古墳（第3次）範囲確認調査|title=明日香村遺跡調査概報 平成9年度|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * その他 ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=[[森浩一]]|title=忘れられていた平田岩屋古墳|date=1971|publisher=[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所]]|journal=青陵|volume=|number=17|naid=|pages=|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=藤井利章|year=1985|chapter=飛鳥谷古墳集団の復原とその歴史的意義|title=末永先生米寿記念献呈論文集 乾|publisher=末永先生米寿記念会|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=亀田博|title=西内成郷と金塚|date=1999|publisher=飛鳥保存財団|journal=季刊明日香風|volume=|number=73|naid=|pages=|ref=}} == 関連項目 == * [[梅山古墳]] * [[吉備姫王]] {{DEFAULTSORT:かなつかこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:陵墓陪冢]]

墳丘 |別名 = 平田岩屋古墳 |所属 = |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 28|緯度秒 = 6.45 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 48|経度秒 = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |形状 = [[方墳]] |規模 = 一辺35m |埋葬施設 = 両袖式[[横穴式石室]] |出土品 = |陪塚 = |築造時期 = [[7世紀]]後半 |被葬者 = （一説）[[吉備姫王]] |陵墓 = 宮内庁治定（「[[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]」の陪冢） |史跡 = |有形文化財 = |特記事項 = |地図 = Japan Nara |アイコン = 方墳 |ラベル = カナヅカ
古墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} '''カナヅカ古墳'''（カナヅカこふん、平田岩屋古墳）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[方墳]]。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、[[梅山古墳]]（[[欽明天皇]]陵）と[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]との間において、丘陵南斜面をコ字形にカットして造成された平坦面上に築造された古墳である。同一丘陵南斜面では、梅山古墳・カナヅカ古墳・鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠・[[野口王墓|野口王墓古墳]]（[[天武天皇|天武]]・[[持統天皇]]合葬陵）の4古墳が東西に並ぶ。本古墳は[[江戸時代]]には石室が開口しており、[[明治]]期には石室が破壊を受けているほか、[[1995年]]度（[[平成]]7年度）以降に範囲確認調査が実施されている{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 墳丘は2段築成{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳形は方形で、一辺約35メートルを測る{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘前面には東西約60メートル・南北約25メートルを測る大型のテラス面が構築される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘周囲には周濠が巡らされており、北側で幅11.7メートルを測る{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。主体部の埋葬施設は両袖式の[[横穴式石室]]で、南方向に開口した（現在は埋没）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室全長約16メートルを測る大型石室であり、石材には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。副葬品は詳らかでない。 このカナヅカ古墳は、[[古墳時代]][[終末期古墳|終末期]]の[[7世紀]]後半頃の築造と推定される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。被葬者は明らかでないが、梅山古墳（欽明天皇陵）との位置関係などから欽明天皇皇孫女の[[吉備姫王]]（[[643年]]死去）の真墓とする説が挙げられている（現墓は梅山古墳南西の塚に治定）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 === 来歴 === * [[江戸時代]] ** [[文化 (元号)|文化]]初年、河内秀根が『書紀集解』に梅山古墳の陪冢の「金冢」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[文政]]12年（[[1829年]]）、津川長道が『卯花日記』に「金塚」として石室露出を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[嘉永]]元年（[[1848年]]）、北浦定政が『打墨縄』に「金塚」として[[吉備姫王]]墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** 嘉永7年（[[1854年]]）、津久井清影が『聖蹟図志』に「岩屋」として石室開口の様子を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[安政]]4年（[[1857年]]）、[[谷森善臣]]の『大和國山陵廻之日記』に「金冢」として吉備姫王墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[慶応]]3年（[[1867年]]）、陵墓図に「字金塚檜隈坂合陵」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1890年]]（[[明治]]23年）、[[三宅米吉]]が破壊に遭遇（[[喜田貞吉]]が記録）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1892年]]（明治25年）、[[宮内省]]（現・[[宮内庁]]）により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1995年|1995]]-[[1997年]]度（[[平成]]7-9年度）、範囲確認調査（明日香村教育委員会、[[1996年|1996]]・1997・[[1998年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1999年]]（平成11年）、明治期の破壊当時の様子を記す資料の発見{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 埋葬施設 == 主体部の埋葬施設としては両袖式[[横穴式石室]]が構築されており、南方向に開口した{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室の規模は次の通り{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * 石室全長：約16メートル * 玄室：長さ約5.45メートル、幅約3.64メートル、高さ約2.7メートル * 羨道：長さ約10メートル、幅約1.8メートル、高さ約1.8メートル 石室には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石の切石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。壁面は玄室では2段積み（奥壁3石：上段1石・下段2石、両側壁4石：上段2石・下段2石）、羨道では1-2段積み（両側壁10石）である{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。また天井石は玄室で2石、羨道で3石から構成される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 石室の形態としては[[岩屋山古墳]]（[[明日香村]]）と同様の特徴を有しており、「岩屋山式石室」と捉えられるが、岩屋山古墳よりも後出して[[峯塚古墳]]（[[天理市]]）からもやや後出する{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * {{Cite 古墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} '''カナヅカ古墳'''（カナヅカこふん、平田岩屋古墳）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[方墳]]。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、[[梅山古墳]]（[[欽明天皇]]陵）と[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]との間において、丘陵南斜面をコ字形にカットして造成された平坦面上に築造された古墳である。同一丘陵南斜面では、梅山古墳・カナヅカ古墳・鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠・[[野口王墓|野口王墓古墳]]（[[天武天皇|天武]]・[[持統天皇]]合葬陵）の4古墳が東西に並ぶ。本古墳は[[江戸時代]]には石室が開口しており、[[明治]]期には石室が破壊を受けているほか、[[1995年]]度（[[平成]]7年度）以降に範囲確認調査が実施されている{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 墳形は方形で、一辺約35メートルを測る{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘は2段築成{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘前面には東西約60メートル・南北約25メートルを測る大型のテラス面が構築される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘周囲には周濠が巡らされており、北側で幅11.7メートルを測る{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。主体部の埋葬施設は両袖式の[[横穴式石室]]で、南方向に開口した（現在は埋没）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室全長約16メートルを測る大型石室であり、石材には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。副葬品は詳らかでない。 このカナヅカ古墳は、[[古墳時代]][[終末期古墳|終末期]]の[[7世紀]]後半頃の築造と推定される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。被葬者は明らかでないが、梅山古墳（欽明天皇陵）との位置関係などから欽明天皇皇孫女の[[吉備姫王]]（[[643年]]死去）の真墓とする説が挙げられている（現墓は梅山古墳南西の塚に治定）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 === 来歴 === * [[江戸時代]] ** [[文化 (元号)|文化]]初年、河内秀根が『書紀集解』に梅山古墳の陪冢の「金冢」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[文政]]12年（[[1829年]]）、津川長道が『卯花日記』に「金塚」として石室露出を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[嘉永]]元年（[[1848年]]）、北浦定政が『打墨縄』に「金塚」として[[吉備姫王]]墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** 嘉永7年（[[1854年]]）、津久井清影が『聖蹟図志』に「岩屋」として石室開口の様子を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[安政]]4年（[[1857年]]）、[[谷森善臣]]の『大和國山陵廻之日記』に「金冢」として吉備姫王墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[慶応]]3年（[[1867年]]）、陵墓図に「字金塚檜隈坂合陵」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1890年]]（[[明治]]23年）、[[三宅米吉]]が破壊に遭遇（[[喜田貞吉]]が記録）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1892年]]（明治25年）、[[宮内省]]（現・[[宮内庁]]）により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1995年|1995]]-[[1997年]]度（[[平成]]7-9年度）、範囲確認調査（明日香村教育委員会、[[1996年|1996]]・1997・[[1998年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1999年]]（平成11年）、明治期の破壊当時の様子を記す資料の発見{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 埋葬施設 == 主体部の埋葬施設としては両袖式[[横穴式石室]]が構築されており、南方向に開口した{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室の規模は次の通り{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * 石室全長：約16メートル * 玄室：長さ約5.45メートル、幅約3.64メートル、高さ約2.7メートル * 羨道：長さ約10メートル、幅約1.8メートル、高さ約1.8メートル 石室には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石の切石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。壁面は玄室では2段積み（奥壁3石：上段1石・下段2石、両側壁4石：上段2石・下段2石）、羨道では1-2段積み（両側壁10石）である{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。また天井石は玄室で2石、羨道で3石から構成される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 石室の形態としては[[岩屋山古墳]]（[[明日香村]]）と同様の特徴を有しており、「岩屋山式石室」と捉えられるが、岩屋山古墳よりも後出して[[峯塚古墳]]（[[天理市]]）からもやや後出する{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * {{Cite 創刊号]}}|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|西光慎治|2000}}}} - リンクは明日香村ホームページ。 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=岡林孝作|year=2002|chapter=カナヅカ古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典|続 日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * 調査報告 ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=[[森浩一]]|title=忘れられていた平田岩屋古墳|date=1971|publisher=[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所]]|journal=青陵|volume=|number=17|naid=|pages=|ref=}} ** {{Cite 墳丘 |別名 = 平田岩屋古墳 |所属 = |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 28|緯度秒 = 6.45 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 48|経度秒 = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |形状 = [[方墳]] |規模 = 一辺35m |埋葬施設 = 両袖式[[横穴式石室]] |出土品 = |陪塚 = |築造時期 = [[7世紀]]後半 |被葬者 = （一説）[[吉備姫王]] |陵墓 = 宮内庁治定（「[[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]」の陪冢） |史跡 = |有形文化財 = |特記事項 = |地図 = Japan Nara |アイコン = 方墳 |ラベル = カナヅカ
古墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} '''カナヅカ古墳'''（カナヅカこふん、平田岩屋古墳）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]平田にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[方墳]]。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、[[梅山古墳]]（[[欽明天皇]]陵）と[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]との間において、丘陵南斜面をコ字形にカットして造成された平坦面上に築造された古墳である。同一丘陵南斜面では、梅山古墳・カナヅカ古墳・鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠・[[野口王墓|野口王墓古墳]]（[[天武天皇|天武]]・[[持統天皇]]合葬陵）の4古墳が東西に並ぶ。本古墳は[[江戸時代]]には石室が開口しており、[[明治]]期には石室が破壊を受けているほか、[[1995年]]度（[[平成]]7年度）以降に範囲確認調査が実施されている{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 墳形は方形で、一辺約35メートルを測る{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘は2段築成{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘前面には東西約60メートル・南北約25メートルを測る大型のテラス面が構築される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。墳丘周囲には周濠が巡らされており、北側で幅11.7メートルを測る{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。埋葬施設は両袖式の[[横穴式石室]]で、南方向に開口した（現在は埋没）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室全長約16メートルを測る大型石室であり、石材には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。副葬品は詳らかでない。 このカナヅカ古墳は、[[古墳時代]][[終末期古墳|終末期]]の[[7世紀]]後半頃の築造と推定される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。被葬者は明らかでないが、梅山古墳（欽明天皇陵）との位置関係などから欽明天皇皇孫女の[[吉備姫王]]（[[643年]]死去）の真墓とする説が挙げられている（現墓は梅山古墳南西の塚に治定）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 遺跡歴 == * [[江戸時代]] ** [[文化 (元号)|文化]]初年、河内秀根が『書紀集解』に梅山古墳の陪冢の「金冢」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[文政]]12年（[[1829年]]）、津川長道が『卯花日記』に「金塚」として石室露出を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[嘉永]]元年（[[1848年]]）、北浦定政が『打墨縄』に「金塚」として[[吉備姫王]]墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** 嘉永7年（[[1854年]]）、津久井清影が『聖蹟図志』に「岩屋」として石室開口の様子を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[安政]]4年（[[1857年]]）、[[谷森善臣]]の『大和國山陵廻之日記』に「金冢」として吉備姫王墓とする説を記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 ** [[慶応]]3年（[[1867年]]）、陵墓図に「字金塚檜隈坂合陵」として記載{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1890年]]（[[明治]]23年）、[[三宅米吉]]が破壊に遭遇（[[喜田貞吉]]が記録）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * [[1892年]]（明治25年）、[[宮内省]]（現・[[宮内庁]]）により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1995年|1995]]-[[1997年]]度（[[平成]]7-9年度）、範囲確認調査（明日香村教育委員会、[[1996年|1996]]・1997・[[1998年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}{{Sfn|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}。 * [[1999年]]（平成11年）、明治期の破壊当時の様子を記す資料の発見{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 埋葬施設 == 埋葬施設としては両袖式[[横穴式石室]]が構築されており、南方向に開口した{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。石室の規模は次の通り{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 * 石室全長：約16メートル * 玄室：長さ約5.45メートル、幅約3.64メートル、高さ約2.7メートル * 羨道：長さ約10メートル、幅約1.8メートル、高さ約1.8メートル 石室には[[石英閃緑岩]]の巨石の切石が使用される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。壁面は玄室では2段積み（奥壁3石：上段1石・下段2石、両側壁4石：上段2石・下段2石）、羨道では1-2段積み（両側壁10石）である{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。また天井石は玄室で2石、羨道で3石から構成される{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 石室の形態としては[[岩屋山古墳]]（[[明日香村]]）と同様の特徴を有しており、「岩屋山式石室」と捉えられるが、岩屋山古墳よりも後出して[[峯塚古墳]]（[[天理市]]）からもやや後出する{{Sfn|西光慎治|2000}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用した文献）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=西光慎治|year=2000|chapter=飛鳥地域の地域史研究(1) 檜隈坂合陵・陪冢 カナヅカ古墳の覚書|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf 明日香村文化財調査研究紀要 創刊号]}}|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|西光慎治|2000}}}} - リンクは明日香村ホームページ。 * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=岡林孝作|year=2002|chapter=カナヅカ古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典|続 日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|カナヅカ古墳（続古墳）|2002}}}} == 関連文献 == {{small|（記事執筆に使用していない関連文献）}} * 調査報告 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=カナヅカ古墳（第1次）範囲確認調査|title=明日香村遺跡調査概報 平成7年度|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=カナヅカ古墳（第2次）範囲確認調査|title=明日香村遺跡調査概報 平成8年度|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=カナヅカ古墳（第3次）範囲確認調査|title=明日香村遺跡調査概報 平成9年度|publisher=明日香村教育委員会|isbn=|ref=}} * その他 ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=[[森浩一]]|title=忘れられていた平田岩屋古墳|date=1971|publisher=[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所]]|journal=青陵|volume=|number=17|naid=|pages=|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=藤井利章|year=1985|chapter=飛鳥谷古墳集団の復原とその歴史的意義|title=末永先生米寿記念献呈論文集 乾|publisher=末永先生米寿記念会|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=亀田博|title=西内成郷と金塚|date=1999|publisher=飛鳥保存財団|journal=季刊明日香風|volume=|number=73|naid=|pages=|ref=}} == 関連項目 == * [[梅山古墳]] * [[吉備姫王]] {{DEFAULTSORT:かなつかこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] revision.text:
{{Ancient Kofun of Japan |Name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |Image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other Name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ** Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ** 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ** 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ** 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ** 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ** 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka]] [[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleum associated tombs]] [[ja:カナヅカ古墳]]

The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ** Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ** 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ** 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ** 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ** 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ** 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ** {{Ancient Kofun of Japan |Name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |Image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other Name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ** Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ** 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ** 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ** 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ** 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ** 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ** While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ** Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ** 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ** 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ** 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ** 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ** 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ** {{Ancient Kofun of Japan |Name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other Name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised {{Ancient Infobox ancient site |Name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other Name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka {{Ancient Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * {{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka {{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised
