Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granddaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granqqaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka {{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granqqaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granqqaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka {{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square burial mound |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granqqaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square Kofun |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granqqaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a granqqaughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square Kofun |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a gran11aughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square Kofun |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a gran11aughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square Kofun |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a gran11aughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} |name = Kanazuka Kofun (カナヅカ古墳) |image = [[File:Kanazuka Kofun (Asuka), funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound |Other name = Hirata Iwaya Kofun |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}{{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 6.45 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 48|Longitude_sec = 12.32 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 35m per side |Burial Chamber = Bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} |Artifacts = |Associated Tombs = |Era = Late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} |Buried Person = (Speculated) {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum) |Cultural Property = |Special Notes = |Map = Japan Nara |Icon = Square Kofun |Label = Kanazuka
Kofun |Label_Position = top }} '''Kanazuka Kofun''' (カナヅカ古墳), also known as Hirata Iwaya Kofun, is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Hirata, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is square-shaped and designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as an associated tomb of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}}. == Overview == Situated on the southern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|奈良盆地}}, the Kanazuka Kofun was constructed on a flattened area cut into the southern slope of a hill between the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Emperor Kinmei’s tomb) and {{ill|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Senkan|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. Along the same hill slope, four kofun align east to west: the Umemiyama Kofun, Kanazuka Kofun, Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Sekishoin, and the {{ill|Noguchi Oukou Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳}} (the joint tomb of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the stone chamber of Kanazuka Kofun was already exposed. However, the chamber suffered significant destruction in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, and from 1995 onward, surveys were conducted to confirm the extent of the site. The kofun is square-shaped, measuring about 35 meters per side, and consists of two tiers. A large terrace measuring approximately 60 meters east-west and 25 meters north-south was constructed in front of the mound. A surrounding moat measuring 11.7 meters in width was identified on the northern side. The burial chamber is a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with an opening to the south. The chamber has a total length of approximately 16 meters, and its stones are made of massive cut pieces of quartz diorite. The Kanazuka Kofun is estimated to date to the late {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}, during the terminal phase of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. While the identity of the interred individual is uncertain, the close proximity to the Umemiyama Kofun has led to speculation that it is the actual burial site of {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}}, a gran11aughter of Emperor Kinmei who died in 643. == History == * {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} ''' Early {{ill|Bunka era|ja|文化 (元号)}}, Hideane Kawauchi mentioned the Kanazuka as an associated tomb in the ''Shugishūkai''. ''' 1829 (Bunsei 12): Nagamichi Tsukawa described the exposed stone chamber as "Kanazuka" in the ''Ubana Diary''. ''' 1848 (Kaei 1): Sadamasa Kitanoura suggested in ''Uchiuminaru'' that the site is the tomb of Princess Kibihime. ''' 1854 (Kaei 7): Kiyokage Tsukui described the open stone chamber as "Iwaya" in the ''Seiseki Zushi''. ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} (in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}). == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinji Saiko|year=2000|chapter=Study of the History of Asuka Region (1): Notes on the Kanazuka Kofun, Associated Tomb of Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum|title={{PDFlink|[https://asukamura.jp/chosa_hokoku/kiyo/imgs/01.pdf Asuka Village Cultural Property Research Bulletin, Vol. 1]}}|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref={{Harvid|Saiko|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Kosaku Okabayashi|year=2002|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun|title={{ill|Revised Encyclopedia of Infobox ancient site|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Kanazuka Kofun (Revised Encyclopedia)|2002}}}} == Related Reading == * Reports ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1996|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 1) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1995|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1997|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 2) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1996|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1998|chapter=Kanazuka Kofun (Phase 3) Boundary Survey|title=Preliminary Report on Asuka Village Archaeological Surveys, 1997|publisher=Asuka Village Board of Education|ref=}} * Other ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Kōichi Mori|title=Forgotten Hirata Iwaya Kofun|date=1971|publisher=Nara Prefecture Kashihara Archaeological Research Institute|journal=Seiryō|volume=|number=17|ref=}} ''' {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Toshiaki Fujii|year=1985|chapter=Reconstruction and Historical Significance of the Asuka Valley Kofun Cluster|title=Essays in Honor of Dr. Suenaga's 88th Birthday|publisher=Suenaga Memorial Committee|ref=}} ''' {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiroshi Kameda|title=Nishiuchiseigō and Kanazuka|date=1999|publisher=Asuka Preservation Foundation|journal=Quarterly Asuka Breeze|volume=|number=73|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} * {{ill|Princess Kibihime|ja|吉備姫王}} Kofun]][[sn:Kanazuka Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kanazuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kanazuka Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanazuka Kofun]][[Category:qq]] ''' 1857 (Ansei 4): Yoshitomi Tanimori speculated it was Princess Kibihime's tomb in the ''Yamato Province Mausoleum Tour Diary''. ''' 1867 (Keiō 3): The site appeared in an Imperial Mausoleum map labeled "Kanazuka, Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum". * 1890 (Meiji 23): Beikichi Miyake witnessed destruction of the stone chamber, as noted by Sadakichi Kida. * 1892 (Meiji 25): The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designated the Kanazuka Kofun as an associated tomb of the {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum). * 1995–1997 (Heisei 7–9): Site boundary surveys were conducted by the Asuka Village Board of Education, with preliminary reports published in 1996, 1997, and 1998. * 1999 (Heisei 11): Records from the Meiji period documenting the destruction of the chamber were discovered. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a bilateral {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} opening southward. The chamber dimensions are as follows: * Total length: approx. 16 meters * Main chamber: approx. 5.45 meters long, 3.64 meters wide, 2.7 meters high * Passageway: approx. 10 meters long, 1.8 meters wide, 1.8 meters high The chamber features massive stones of cut quartz diorite. The main chamber walls are constructed in two tiers (three stones for the rear wall and four for each side wall), while the passageway walls are composed of one or two tiers (10 stones for each side). The ceiling consists of two stones in the main chamber and three in the passageway. The chamber design is similar to the "Iwayayama-style" stone chamber found in the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} (also in Asuka), though it is slightly later and precedes the construction of
